text: Laparoscopic gastric seromyotomy and conversion to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass for management of proximal stricture after vertical sleeve gastrectomy. We have no financial disclosures. Our patient is a forty-four-year-old female who was referred to us for severe gastroesophageal reflux symptoms. Her past history was significant for a laparoscopic vertical sleeve gastrectomy about four years ago that was complicated by a stricture at the proximal sleeve. This was treated with stent, which was then later removed, and balloon dilation. She also had an episode of necrotizing aspiration pneumonia requiring VATS decortication. An upper GI series is seen here that shows severe stricture at the proximal sleeve. Endoscopy showed severely dilated esophagus with esophagitis. This picture shows distally dilated esophagus with severe esophagitis and food debris. Z line with esophagitis. This shows dilated proximal sleeve with food debris. And a retroflexed view of proximal sleeve stricture. Here we can see a large hiatal hernia, part of proximal sleeve in the hernia sac.

The surgeon begins by dissecting the pars flaccida and right crus, proceeding anteriorly to the esophagus. The dissection continues to the neogradial curvature off the sleeve and extends to the left crus. Posterior dissection of the esophagus is performed, and a Penrose drain is placed for retraction. The pylorus is clamped to prepare for intraoperative endoscopy, which is used to localize the stricture. The stricture is not clearly visible laparoscopically, but its location is indicated by a grasper, with potential extrinsic compression from scar tissue observed.

The surgeon navigates the endoscope past the stricture at the proximal sleeve, followed by dissection of scar tissue from the sleeve. Despite this, the stricture remains unchanged. Instruments demonstrate the proposed seromyotomy area, where the surgeon dissects longitudinal muscle fibers and extends the myotomy proximally and distally, revealing circular muscle fibers and creating a submucosal plane.

The surgeon performs endoscopy to confirm the completion of the seromyotomy, with visible bulging of the submucosa. The hiatal hernia is repaired by re-approximating the crura posteriorly. A gastric pouch is formed, and a jejunojejunostomy is created using a linear cutting stapler. The mesenteric defect is closed with non-absorbable sutures, and an anti-obstructive Brolin stitch is placed to prevent kinking of the jejunum at the jejunojejunostomy site.

The surgeon creates a gastrojejunostomy, sewing a continuous seromuscular posterior layer with non-absorbable suture, followed by an inner full-thickness layer with running vicryl suture. The seromuscular layer is completed with running non-absorbable suture. An air leak test is performed, showing no leaks from the seromyotomy site or gastrojejunostomy. The Peterson defect is closed with running non-absorbable suture, and the anterior defect at the hiatus is closed with interrupted non-absorbable suture. The seromyotomy area is inspected and covered with omentum.

Patient had a normal post-operative recovery. Symptoms of nausea and reflux were improved immediately. Endoscopic guided laparoscopic seromyotomy and revision to Roux-en-Y gastric bypass is an effective treatment for proximal gastric sleeve strictures that have failed balloon divation instant.
